Corrects previous tornado report from 4 SE Marion. On January 5, 2025 an EF2 tornado moved through portions of Union Parish in Louisiana. The tornado began on Cable Lane Road, southeast of Marion at approximately 20:19. The tornado then continues to move northeast, crossing Boothville Road while snapping numerous tree branches and tree trunks, some of which covered the roadway. By 20:22, the tornado reached Earnest Pace Road, where a trailer was lifted and blown off of its foundation, with associated debris also being blown across the road. Extensive vehicle damage was also noted in this area. The tornado continued to move northeast, producing damage along Jurd Pardue Road, where several homes also experienced damage to roofs and carports. The tornado then crossed Dean Church Road where the most extensive damage was found. A local shop collapsed and was then blown off of its foundation. This is where estimated peak winds of 132 mph were found. The tornado then moved along Parish Road 2260, producing damage to another homes roof and nearby sheds before lifting into the Upper Ouachita Wildlife Refuge. Additional damage was located using high-resolution Sentinel-2 satellite data, although this area was inaccessible by ground. Based on the satellite imagery, the tornado continued northeast and crossed the Ouachita River into Morehouse Parish. The updated track length in the WFO SHV CWA is 7.54 miles.

On January 5, 2025 an EF2 tornado moved through portions of Union Parish in Louisiana. The tornado began on Cable Lane Road, southeast of Marion at approximately 20:19. The tornado then continues to move northeast, crossing Boothville Road while snapping numerous tree branches and tree trunks, some of which covered the roadway. By 20:22, the tornado reached Earnest Pace Road, where a trailer was lifted and blown off of its foundation, with associated debris also being blown across the road. Extensive vehicle damage was also noted in this area. The tornado continued to move northeast, producing damage along Jurd Pardue Road, where several homes also experienced damage to roofs and carports. The tornado then crossed Dean Church Road where the most extensive damage was found. A local shop collapsed and was then blown off of its foundation. This is where estimated peak winds of 132 mph were found. The tornado then moved along Parish Road 2260, producing damage to another homes roof and nearby sheds before lifting into the Upper Quachita Wildlife Refuge.

*** 3 INJ *** An EF1 Tornado with winds as high as 104 mph first touched down across southern Franklin County Texas, travelling east northeast across the southern shore of Lake Bob Sandlin bordering southern Titus County and northern Camp County before lifting near Hwy 271 due east of the Lake Bob Sandlin Dam. This tornado uprooted and snapped several trees along its path. Some roof damage was observed to several structures along with partial roof removal of others. Three injuries occurred when RV campers were overturned at a campground. Widest pathwidth of this tornado was 858 yards wide.
Storm spotter report of trees down near 344 and 346 (Teaselville, TX).
Spotter reported power line down on top of a car near intersection of Kinsey Dr and Loop 323 (Tyler, TX).

Report from mPING: 1-inch tree limbs broken; Shingles blown off.
Tree down on a vehicle in Cason.

A NWS Storm Survey concluded that damage across Morris County was consistent with that of an EF-1 Tornado with maximum wind gusts near 88 mph. The tornado first touched down near FM 144, north of Hwy 49 with the storm traveling east northeast, uprooting and/or snapping several trees along its path. The tornado eventually crossed Hwy. 259 just north of Rocky Branch and lifted near Daniels Chapel Road near the Morris County, Cass County border.

A NWS Storm Survey concluded that damage across extreme eastern Red River County, and Bowie County in Northeast Texas was consistent with an EF-1 Tornado with maximum wind gusts near 100 mph. The tornado first touched down on FM 44 near the community of Lydia, Texas. Numerous trees were uprooted and snapped along its track as the tornado travelled east northeast. The worst damage was found near the Barry B Telford Unit where a shed was destroyed along with several trailers overturned and/or significantly damaged. The tornado finally lifted near the intersection of CR 3014 and FM 3378 or northeast of Malta, Texas.

A NWS Storm Survey concluded that damage across Bowie County was consistent with an EF-1 Tornado with maximum wind gusts near 110 mph. This tornado first began along Hwy. 259 in far southwest Bowie County near the Sulphur River. This tornado travelled east northeast for nearly 20 miles before lifting along the south side of New Boston or near the Red River Army Depot. The tornado snapped and uprooted several trees along its path. Some structures were damaged as well including the metal roof covering from a home and other metal frame structures.
Report from mPING: 3-inch tree limbs broken; Power poles broken.
Reports of roofs taken off houses in De Kalb. No specific locations given.
Reports of structures damaged in New Boston.

This brief EF-1 tornado started in the community of Scottsville, east of the city of Marshall. Damage associated with this tornado was almost exclusively limited to trees. The tornado had a path length of approximately 1 mile, peak width of 100 yards, and maximum estimated wind speed of 90 mph.
Howard County Sheriff Office received report of a tree limb down on a home on W Sunset St in Nashville.

This EF1 tornado began just east of the Texas/Louisiana State Line in Caddo Parish and generally moved east along Blanchard Furrh Road. Most of the damage consisted of snapped and uprooted softwood trees and a couple of snapped hardwood trees. A metal truss tower and a pump station was bent in half to the ground. The tower appeared to have been twisted from the north and then bent towards the east. The tornado lifted just before the intersection of Blanchard Furrh Road and Springwood Lane. Path length was 3.91 miles. Max width was 114 yards. Peak est. winds were 93 mph.
Several structures damaged in the Nine Forks community, including two rolled single wide mobile homes, due to straight line winds estimated to be 70 to 90 mph.
Hempstead County Sheriff Office received a report of trees down on Hempstead 185 near HwY 278 W just outside of the town of Old Washington.
Reports of fallen tree limbs and damage to several chicken houses at the intersection of CR 19 and CR 21.
Report from mPING: Roof blown off.
Measured at the Angelina County Airport near Burke.

This brief EF1 tornado touched down in the North Highlands neighborhood of North Shreveport. It initially began along Booth Drive and move east snapping the trunks of a couple of hardwood trees and downing large limbs from softwood trees. A few metal roof panels were also removed from an outbuilding LA Hwy 3049/Dixie Shreveport Road. The tornado end point is placed here as no additional damage was located. Much of the land between this point and the Red River consisted of open fields. No consistent damage track was located east of the Red River in Bossier Parish. Path length was 0.92 miles. Max. width was 225 yards. Peak est. winds were 93 mph.
Measured at WFO SHV by ASOS.

This brief but relatively intense EF-1 tornado tracked for 2 miles across rural portions of southwestern Claiborne Parish, doing mainly tree damage. Fortunately, there were no injuries. Peak wind speed was 110 mph and maximum width was 300 yards.
Reports of trees down throughout Huxley.

A strong EF-1 tornado with estimated maximum winds near 105 mph touched down in the Sabine National Forest along Forest Road 144, and tracked northeast across the forest and Highway 87 just south of the Six Mile community. The tornado was strongest near the intersection of Forest Road 144 and Forest Road 144A, where numerous softwood trees were snapped and uprooted. Additional trees were snapped and uprooted as it crossed Highway 87 between Forest Road 182 and Shawnee Shores Drive, before eventually lifting in a cove of Toledo Bend Reservoir near Shawnee Shores Drive. The estimated path length was 5.71 miles, with a path width of 625 yards.
Clustering of trees down in USFS land. Possible tornado.
Report of a large tree uprooted on a house at the intersection of Sixth St and Calion Rd.
Reports of trees and powerlines down, concentrated in area surrounding Shawnee Shore Rd.
Trees down with one fallen over Highway 3121.

Corrects previous tornado report from 13 NW Columbia on March 15 at 13:06Z. Upon further investigation and consultation with high resolution satellite imagery, this previously reported very small and brief tornado produced tree damage that is now deemed to be due to straight line thunderstorm winds on March 4 at 17:54Z.
Report from mPING: 3-inch tree limbs broken; Power poles broken.

Corrects previous tornado report from 6 SSE Clarks occurring on March 15 at 13:21Z. Further investigation has informed that this tornado actually occurred on March 04 at 18:10Z. This brief EF-1 tornado crossed near the intersection of Hammock Road and Highway 506 in the very southern portion of Caldwell Parish. Damage was mostly to trees, although a few structures received lower end damage. Peak winds were 100 mph and width was 200 yards. The path length was limited to approximately half a mile due to ground access limiting survey access by the team.
Multiple trees down on homes on Mary Street just north of Natchitoches.

*** 2 INJ *** NWS Storm Survey determined that damage across southern Hempstead County was consistent with that of an EF1 tornado with maximum wind speeds near 100 mph and a maximum path width of 525 yds. Damage consisted of numerous trees uprooted and/or snapped along its track. The tornado tracked east northeast, crossing Hwy 29S where damage to the roofing of several structures was witnessed. A camper and cattle trailer were thrown nearly 100 yards east of Hwy. 29S. Off of CR 150, a mobile home was completely destroyed, injuring 2 residents inside. The tornado finally lifted as it approached Hwy. 32 just west of the Hempstead/Nevada County line.
Tree reported down on Hwy 29 south of Hope, AR.
*** 1 INJ *** Trailer house blown over south of Hope, AR. One person taken to the hospital.
Damage to a roof and 4-wheelers, destroyed and flipped storage buildings, all near a home on Hempstead 57 Rd.

NWS Storm Survey determined that damage across Nevada County was consistent with that of an EF2 tornado with maximum wind speeds near 115 mph and a maximum path width near 700 yards. Damage consisted of numerous trees uprooted and/or snapped along its track. The tornado tracked northeast near the intersection of CR 432 and CR 45 damage was witnessed to several small barns and outbuildings. A stretch of highway between CR 47 and 423 saw extensive damage to trees, some of which fell onto several homes. The tornado reached its peak intensity in this area. The tornado appeared to have lifted near White Oak Lake as it moved near the Ouachita County, Arkansas line.
Multiple trees down across roads, including Hwy 24 east of Prescott, AR.

This weak and brief EF-1 tornado crossed over Highway 4 and produced a small area of convergent tree damage typical of a small and weak tornado. Ground access was very limited for the survey team, although high res satellite may be used to get more insight into the track when this data becomes available. Peak winds were 88 mph and peak width around 25 yards with the accessible path length only around 100 yards.

This tornado began SE of Shelbyville near the Hurstown community at the intersection of TX Hwy 87 and CR 2130. Most of the damage consisted of snapped and uprooted trees, but a few chicken houses at the tornado start point lost a few metal roof panels. The tornado generally moved north and lifted near the intersection of FS Road 143 and FM 417. Peak winds were 95 mph. Path length was 6.13 miles. Max. width was 472 yards.

A tornado began just south of HWY 20 between Vivian and Hosston and then crossed Black Bayou Lake. After crossing the Lake, tree damage was found on the north-central side of the Lake along Rudd Rd. Additional damage was then found on Mira Mrytis Rd and along Atlanta Mira Rd before the tornado lifted. The tornado lasted about 20 minutes for just under 9 miles.

An EF-1 tornado with estimated maximum winds near 110 mph touched down along the Red River bordering Miller and Lafayette Counties, and tracked northeast across CR 57, snapping and uprooted several trees. The tornado then tracked over open farmland for several miles before crossing Highway 160 just west of Gin City, where 7 wooden power poles were snapped. The tornado continued northeast crossing Highway 360 where it snapped 3 more wooden power poles and 2 trees before lifting. The tornado path length was 7.81 miles with a path width of 250 yards.
AR State Hwy 160 near Gin City is blocked by downed power lines and power poles. Potential tornado.

A brief EF-1 tornado with estimated maximum winds near 95 mph touched down in a wooded area just west of Highway 53, where multiple trees were snapped and uprooted. The tornado crossed Highway 53 where additional trees were snapped and uprooted, before lifting. The tornado patch length was 0.28 miles with a path width of 75 yards.
Public photo of Tornado near Lake Columbia.

Report of confirmed tornado and damage in Rosalie community just east of Bogata.

An NWS Storm Survey team determined that damage across the southwest portion of Red River County, Texas was consistent with that of EF-2 tornado damage with maximum winds near 130 mph. The tornado began northeast of Bogata along CR 1305. The storm moved northeast towards Clarksville where it uprooted and/or snapped several trees along its path. A home north of CR 1325 suffered major damage. Other outbuildings were damaged or destroyed as well. As the tornado moved across the Clarksville/Red River County Airport, the storm collapsed the roof of a larger hanger and damaged several planes before lifting.

Corrects previous tornado report from 3 NE Bogata. An NWS Storm Survey team determined that damage across the southwest portion of Red River County, Texas was consistent with that of EF-2 tornado damage with maximum winds near 130 mph. The tornado began northeast of Bogata along CR 1305. The storm moved northeast towards Clarksville where it uprooted and/or snapped several trees along its path. A home north of CR 1325 suffered major damage. Other outbuildings were damaged or destroyed as well. The tornado track just east of Clarksville and lifted just south of the Red River. Total path length was 21.54 miles.
EMS reports damage to trees and powerlines near the intersection of Snows Arena Road and US Highway 70.

A tornado touched down just south of HWY 70, uprooting and snapping trees while also damaging a small outbuilding/shed next to a home. The tornado cross the HWY and then produced extensive tree damage and some structural damage along Quails Nest Rd before heading into the Cedar Mountains. The tornado then continued across North Pole, OK, producing extensive tree damage along Pine View Rd before traveling toward Old Glory Mountain. The tornado would then continue NE and cross HWY 259 north of Hochatown before lifting, producing additional tree damage.

An EF-1 tornado, with estimated maximum winds near 110 mph, touched down on the far eastern side of Clarksville along FM 1159 just north of East Main Street, where a hardwood tree was snapped. The tornado then tracked northeast across Highway 82, across a pasture and over CR 3200/along FM 1700, where multiple large tree branches were snapped. The tornado intensified and was strongest as it entered the Madras community, where numerous large trees were snapped along FM 1700. After moving over a heavily wooded area along Little White Oak Creek, the tornado generally tracked north along CR 3230, where additional trees were snapped and uprooted, before lifting shortly before crossing CR 3235. The tornado path length was 10.1 miles, with a path length of around 410 yards.
Report of a house destroyed on Farm to Market Rd 909 from local sheriff.
Report of confirmed tornado on ground near the Talco community.
Dime to quarter size hail reported by Amateur Radio operator.
Damage reported at intersection of CR 1345 and CR 1360.

*** 1 INJ *** Updated start point from previous tornado report from 3 W Hawkins. An NWS Storm Survey determined that damage across northeast Smith, southeast Wood, and western Upshur Counties was consistent with that of an EF-1 tornado. Tree damage was first observed near U.S. Hwy. 80. Downstream damage consisted of numerous trees snapped and/or uprooted along its near 20 mile track. Some structures also received roof damage along the track. One area of significant roof damage was observed to a water bottling plant north of Hawkins, Tx. One injury occurred to a driver in a semi-truck at the bottling plant.

*** 1 INJ *** An NWS Storm Survey determined that damage across southeast Wood and western Upshur County was consistent with that of an EF-1 tornado. Tree damage was first observed near U.S. Hwy. 80. Downstream damage consisted of numerous trees snapped and/or uprooted along its near 20 mile track. Some structures also received roof damage along the track. One area of significant roof damage was observed to a water bottling plant north of Hawkins, Tx. One injury occurred to a driver in a semi-truck at the bottling plant.
Pictures of damage at a manufacturing plant, coincident with a tornadic debris signature. Time estimated by radar.
Horse loafing shed roof and walls damage and trees down on Kelsey Creek Rd and Cox trail, just south of State Hwy 154 in the Kelsey community west of Gilmer.
Photo of destroyed residence in the Kelsey community along State Highway 154.
Damage to bridge road surface on FM Road 852 over Lake Gilmer.
Trees uprooted on the south side of Lake Gilmer. Initially reported by public. NWS Survey Team determined this to have been caused by RFD winds.
Carport lifted and damaged East end of Barber Rd in the Bettie community.
Structural damage at Union Hill elementary. Possible tornado.
Multiple trees down on FM Road 2088, West of US Highway 271, between the Union Hill and Bettie communities. Also, some structure damage from trees.

An NWS Storm Survey determined that damage across western Upshur County was consistent with that of a short tracked EF-1 tornado. While the tornado was brief, a few hardwoods and softwood trees were snapped and uprooted along the track.
Pictures of major street flooding in Lindale.

An EF2 tornado began SW of Lone Star and moved NE across Ellison Creek Reservoir and across the north side of Lone Star, dissipating along FM 3421. Most of the damage consisted of snapped and uprooted trees. Dozens of homes in Lone Star sustained damage ranging from minor to severe from the falling trees. EF2 damage was noted to a house on the western shore of the lake and in a concentrated area of snapped pine trees where 6 cars were rolled/thrown approx. 100 yards. Est. peak winds were 120 mph. Path length was 4.09 yards. Max. width was 673 yards.
Trees snapped, overturned cars, and additional damage near the Lakeview Baptist Assembly in Lone Star.
Flooding on South Texas Street in Pittsburg.
Water over Highway 37, just south of Bogata.
KTBS-TV viewer reports golf ball size hail just east of Maud community near Wright Patman Lake.

Flash flooding in Texarkana. Water over exit 220 on I-30 Westbound.
Corrects previous tstm wnd dmg report from 3 NE Garland. (Storm damage was from 8:00pm on 4/4.) Report of multiple power poles down on CR 5.
Possible Tornado damage near intersection of Highway 196 and Highway 134. Up to 7 power poles snapped.
Reports of trees down and other damage along Highway 82 near Garland. Time estimated by radar. Possible tornado.
Water over Main Street and other side streets across Atlanta.

This relatively brief EF1 tornado originated from a hybrid supercell thunderstorm moving quickly across South-Central Arkansas on the evening on April 4th, 2025. The tornado first touched down along Highway 32 just west of the Hempstead and Nevada County line, quickly moving northeast into Nevada County. The survey team acknowledges the tornado could have actually begun southwest of this point, but there was a lack of ground access for quite a distance upstream of this point. After moving into Nevada County, the tornado produced primarily tree damage as it passed across portions of Nevada 111 and Nevada 13, ending before it crossed County Road 11 to the northeast. Along the tornado’s path, a majority of the damage was to hardwood and softwood trees, although a few structures were damaged, including some chicken houses near the end of the tornado’s path. Peak width was 375 yards, peak wind was 105 mph, and path length was nearly 3 miles.
Water over Lake Placid Road in Tyler.
Golf ball size hail reported in the middle of Eagletown.
Just north of the Eagletown community.
Multiple water rescues. Mostly stranded vehicles.
Reports of flooding along I-30 on Texas. Also, flooding on Stateline Avenue.
Widespread Flooding across Miller County. Several roads flooding, along with some evacuations, and high water rescues.
Texarkana PD reports shut down along I-30 from Mile Marker 220 to Mile Marker 223 due to flooding.
Widespread flooding across Hempstead County. Several roads under water, with some washed out. Also, flash floods result in damage to Ozan Creek Bridge Hempstead 26.
Several reports of damage in the area. Higher concentration around Arnold Ln near Red Lick.
Picture of trees down on a house on Old Blackman Ferry Rd.
Several reports of damage across the AR side of Texarkana. No specific area given by dispatcher.
Multiple trees snapped/uprooted and power lines down on Union Rd (from Hwy 71 to Hwy 237).

This EF2 tornado first touched down just north of Interstate Thirty along Hempstead 49 and quickly came to Hempstead 2, which the tornado then traveled down to the east-northeast for much of the tornado’s life. Along the tornado’s nearly 11 mile path, a majority of the damage was to hardwood and softwood trees, although some structures were damaged. Most of the structure damage was to outbuildings and carports, including such a structure belonging to the Hope VFD. There was at least one recreational vehicle rolled with a mobile home pushed off of its foundation. Fortunately, no injuries or fatalities were reported in association with this tornado. Peak wind was 115 mph and peak width was 500 yards.
Report of a tree blocking I-30, near MM 36.
Report of multiple power poles down on CR 5.
Tree downed in Plain Dealing.
I-30 WB right lane lane blocked due to High Water (0.74 travel distance). Highway Police is onsite and traffic is continuing to move slowly.

Multiple county roads washed out, including CR 31. Multiple county roads closed due to high water, including CR 16.

Widespread flash flooding across Nevada County. This includes several county roads. County Road and Bridge departments working to restore infrastructures across the county.

Public reports flooding on Caples Road, which forced vehicle off the roadway.

NWS Storm Survey across southern Smith County, Texas determined that damage was consistent with that of an EF-1 Tornado with peak winds near 93 mph. The tornado uprooted and/or snapped several trees along its near eastward path with the strongest part of the storm near North Houston Street and west of US Hwy. 69 and near a neighborhood just north of Lynch Drive near Bullard, Texas. Several structures along the path of this storm suffered roof damage as well.

An EF-1 tornado with estimated maximum winds near 105 mph touched down in Southwest Caddo Parish north of Springridge Texas Line Road and west of Providence Road, and tracked northeast for nearly 13 miles into Southwest Shreveport. Damage consisted primarily of numerous snapped and uprooted trees, with several homes suffering damage from fallen trees on Adams and Sebastian Roads in Southwest Caddo Parish, as well on Lytham Drive and North Heatherstone Drive in the Newcastle subdivision in Southwest Shreveport. The tornado lifted along Mackey Lane shortly before reaching Summer Grove Baptist Church. The tornado maximum path width was 810 yards.

Corrects previous tornado report from 1 SSE Benton. Around 12:36 PM CDT on September 24, 2025, a brief EF-1 tornado touched down in south Benton, near the Lost River Estates development. The tornado initially blew down a fence in the backyard of a residential home on the south side of the development. It also snapped a softwood tree across Palmetto Road. The tornado then crossed the road where it snapped large tree branches at the entrance of the Bossier Parish Schools Maintenance Palmetto Warehouse. Aside from ripping a road sign out of the ground on the west side of Palmetto Road, the tornado also damaged a residential entrance on the east side of the road. At the same time, a trash can from the Lost River Estates was spotted in the open field on the north side of the warehouse suggesting strong winds. The tornado then lifted abruptly, as no damage was detected at the warehouse or the adjacent barns and homes along Duval Street and James Street access road.
Tree across Research Station Rd.

Around 12:55 PM CDT on September 25, 2025, a brief EF-1 tornado touched down in south Benton, near the Lost River Estates development. The tornado initially blew down a fence in the backyard of a residential home on the south side of the development. It also snapped a softwood tree across Palmetto Road. The tornado then crossed the road where it snapped large tree branches at the entrance of the Bossier Parish Schools Maintenance Palmetto Warehouse. Aside from ripping a road sign out of the ground on the west side of Palmetto Road, the tornado also damaged a residential entrance on the east side of the road. At the same time, a trash can from the Lost River Estates was spotted in the open field on the north side of the warehouse suggesting strong winds. The tornado then lifted abruptly, as no damage was detected at the warehouse or the adjacent barns and homes along Duval Street and James Street access road.

This EF1 tornado began along CR 4114 and generally tracked east just north of FM 2710/CR 452. Most of the damage consisted of snapped and uprooted trees. Several single-family homes sustained significant roof damage. The tornado lifted along CR 4104 just north of FM 2710. Max. est. wind speed was 100 mph. Path length was 4.46 miles. Max. width was 137 yards.
Trees and homes damaged off FM 2170, CR 4126, and CR 4128.
Social Media photo of large snapped tree limbs at a residence in the northeastern section of Nacogdoches.
Damage to trees, powerlines, outbuildings, and the roof of a mobile home on CR 1122 near the Gregg/Rusk County line.

This EF1 tornado began in Gregg County along CR 1122 west of Kilgore. The tornado generally moved east-southeast and into Kilgore and Rusk County. Most of the damage consisted of downed tree limbs and snapped and uprooted trees. Two double-wide manufactured homes near the start point sustained significant roof damage. Two businesses along Industrial Ave. lost most of their roofs. The tornado ended near the intersection of Hunter Street and Broadway Blvd in Kilgore after moving through a residential area. Several homes in this area sustained minor shingle loss. Maximum wind speed is estimated at 94 mph. Path length was 2.56 miles. Maximum width was 169 yards.
Trees and power lines down reported south of Driller Park and R.E. St. Johns Football Stadium.
Tree limbs down on CR 366 east of FM 1798.

Social Media photo of the top of a snapped tree taking down powerlines at a residence in the Jordan Hill community in SE Winn Parish.
Damage reported to a house and a pole barn at TX Hwy 135 and CR 4212 NE of Jacksonville.

Corrects previous tornado report from 3 NE Jacksonville. Shortly after 9pm on November 20, 2025, a brief EF-1 tornado touched down just east of State Highway 135, approximately 3 miles northeast of downtown Jacksonville, TX and 2 miles southwest of Tecula, TX near the intersection of County Road 4212. This tornado continued to move northeast across County Road 4212, destroying a large barn next to a residential home and throwing metal roofing several hundred yards to the northeast. A large tree trunk was punctured by metal debris and nearby trees were snapped along with some downed powerlines. A carport was also thrown on top of a garage and a neighboring manufactured home had its back porch blown off. This tornado then moved northeast through a wooded area to County Road 4213. Another barn was destroyed, along with the roof of a carport blown onto the driveway of a residence. Further north along County Road 4213, there were multiple large uprooted trees and large branches snapped. A nearby manufactured home also displayed significant roof damage. The tornado then lifted within a field nearby the railroad tracks that run north back to State Highway 135. The tornado path length was 0.72 miles with a path width of 120 yards.

Shortly after 9pm on November 20, 2025, a brief EF-1 tornado touched down just east of State Highway 135, approximately 3 miles northeast of downtown Jacksonville, TX and 2 miles southwest of Tecula, TX near the intersection of County Road 4212. This tornado continued to move northeast across County Road 4212, destroying a large barn next to a residential home and throwing metal roofing several hundred yards to the northeast. A large tree trunk was punctured by metal debris and nearby trees were snapped along with some downed powerlines. A carport was also thrown on top of a garage and a neighboring manufactured home had its back porch blown off. This tornado then moved northeast through a wooded area to County Road 4213. Another barn was destroyed, along with the roof of a carport blown onto the driveway of a residence. Further north along County Road 4213, there were multiple large uprooted trees and large branches snapped. A nearby manufactured home also displayed significant roof damage. The tornado then lifted within a field nearby the railroad tracks that run north back to State Highway 135. The tornado patch length was 0.72 miles with a path width of 120 yards.

Widespread flash flooding across the city of Shreveport. Multiple road closures, vehicles stalled in high water along with high water rescues reported across parts of the city as well. Rainfall rates near 2.5 to 3 inches an hour reported with training convection across the city.
Flooding reported near Youree Drive and Kings Highway. Rainfall rates near 3 inches per hour reported in parts of the city of Shreveport.
